q.t:
Consider the following pairs with reference to the pre-historic sites in the Indian subcontinent:
S No.
Site
 Significant finding
1.
 Hathnora
 Only well-known hominin fossil of India
2.
 Burzahom
 Pit dwellings
3.
 Mehrgarh
 Coastal town Harappan period
Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?

so.t:
The earliest age in history is called the Old Stone Age or Palaeolithic. This period is divided into Lower Palaeolithic culture Middle Palaeolithic culture Upper Palaeolithic culture.
o The period after the Old Stone Age (Palaeolithic) is called the Mesolithic Age. The period that followed the Mesolithic is called the Neolithic Age.
o This is the age in which animal and plant domestication developed, leading to food production.
o The classification of these cultures is done on the basis of stratigraphic, chronological and lithic (stone tool) evidence.
Pair 1 is correctly matched:
● Unlike Africa, evidence of hominin (immediate ancestor of Homo Sapiens) fossil is rare in India.
● There is a report of a fossil fragment discovered by Robert Bruce Foote from Athirampakkam. Its whereabouts are not known now.
● The only well-known hominin fossil of India was found at Hathnora near Hoshangabad in Madhya Pradesh. The cranium is named Narmada human.
o A partly preserved hominid skull cap was found in a basal conglomerate deposit in 1982. It is considered to represent the Archaic Homo sapiens.
o It is the only existing fossil find of human ancestors in India.
Pair 2 is correctly matched:
● The Neolithic period marked the beginning of agriculture and animal domestication. It is an important phase in Indian history.
● The Neolithic Culture of Kashmir Neolithic culture in Kashmir region was contemporary to the Harappan civilisation.
● Burzahom, an important site of this culture, provides evidence for the Megalithic and Early Historic Periods. In this place, people lived in pit houses (about four metres in depth) in order to escape the cold weather.
Pair 3 is not correctly matched:
● Mehrgarh, Rana Ghundai, Sarai Kala and Jalilpur are some of the Neolithic sites. These sites are now situated in Pakistan.
● The site of Mehrgarh has produced evidence of early Neolithic times, dating to c. 7000 BCE. Wheat and barley were cultivated and sheep, goat and cattle were domesticated. This culture preceded the Indus Civilisation.
● The people belonging to this age did not use pottery, but cultivated six-row barley, emmer and einkorn wheat, jujube, ilanthai and dates, and also domesticated sheep, goat and cattle.

q.t:
Consider the following pairs:
S No.
Land Revenue System
 Introduced by
1.
 Dahsala system
 Sher Shah Suri
2.
 Zabti System
 Akbar
3.
 Ijara System
 Zulfiqar Khan
Which of the pairs given above is/are correct?

so.t:
● Pair 1 is not correct: In 1580, Akbar introduced a new system called dahsala for land revenue settlement. Under this system, the average produce of different crops as well as the average prices prevailing over the last ten years were calculated. One-third of the average produce was the state’s share. The state demand was however in cash. This was done by considering the state share of money on the basis of a schedule of average prices over the past ten years.
● Pair 2 is not correct: Zabti was the most important method of assessment in Mughal India. The origin of this practice is traced to Sher Shah Suri. During Akbar's reign, the system was revised a number of times before it took the final shape. The main features of the zabti system as it finally came into operation under Akbar were: o measurement of land was essential o fixed cash revenue rates known as dastur for each crop o all the collection was made in cash
● Pair 3 is correct: Ijara system or revenue farming was introduced by Zulfiqar Khan during the reign of Jahandar Shah. The Ijarah system was a revenue farming system that involved assigning land to third parties in exchange for a payment. It was used when peasants were unable to cultivate their land or when a calamity made cultivation impossible. The system was popular in the 18th century for collecting and assessing revenue.

q.t:
If India is experiencing economic growth, then which of the following statements must hold true:
1. Real GDP will increase
2. Nominal GDP will increase
3. Rate of growth of real GDP will increase
4. Increase in employment generation
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
Real GDP growth measures growth in quantity and nominal GDP measures growth in value (which includes quantity as well as price).
Now, suppose an economy produces wheat and rice. The quantities produced and the market price is given in the following table.
Here is the table from the image, formatted in plain text as closely as possible to the original layout:
2011-12
 2012-13
 2013-14
 2014-15
Wheat
 10kg × Rs. 10/kg
 11kg × Rs. 10.5/kg
 12kg × Rs. 11/kg
 12.5kg × Rs. 12/kg
Rice
 8kg × Rs. 12/kg
 9kg × Rs. 12.5/kg
 10kg × Rs. 13/kg
 10.5kg × Rs. 13.5/kg
Nominal GDP
 10×10 + 8×12 = Rs. 196
 11×10.5 + 9×12.5 = Rs. 228
 12×11 + 10×13 = Rs. 262
 12.5×12 + 10.5×13.5 = Rs. 291.75
To calculate Real GDP, we take the price of any year as constant and declare it as a base year. So, suppose we declare 2011-12 as base year then we will take price of wheat as Rs. 10/kg and price of rice as Rs. 12/kg as constant in all the subsequent years to calculate the real GDP.
Real GDP
 10×10 + 8×12 = Rs. 196
 11×10 + 9×12 = Rs. 218
 12×10 + 10×12 = Rs. 240
 12.5×10 + 10.5×12 = Rs. 251
The base year for calculation of GDP is 2011-12. So, if we want to calculate India's Real GDP for 2014-15 then we will have to take the quantities produced in 2014-15 and the prices of 2011-12 (base year). And if we want to calculate the Nominal GDP of 2014-15 then we will have to take the quantities produced in 2014-15 and the market prices of the same year i.e., 2014-15.
In India, economic growth is measured by change in real GDP i.e., GDP at constant Market Prices as per the global best practices.
Consider the above table once again.

So, Real GDP is steadily/consistently increasing from 2011-12 to 2014-15 but "change in real GDP" is decreasing from 11.2% to 4.6% and change in nominal GDP is also decreasing from 2011-12 to 2014-15.
The above is a case of economic growth as real GDP is increasing. But since the rate of change of real GDP is decreasing (but not negative), we say that economic growth is slowing down. When the "real GDP" decreases or "change in real GDP" is negative then it is a case of recession.
And if the prices are decreasing more but the production quantity is increasing then the nominal GDP may decrease but Real GDP may increase. So, in case of economic growth, nominal GDP may decrease.

So, only 1st statement is the required criteria. Because even if 3rd statement is not true (as in the above example), a country may experience economic growth.
Economic growth can happen even without generation of employment, so it is not a required criterion.

q.t:
Consider the following factors:
1. Low pressure over the Tibetan Plateau.
2. High temperatures in the Western Ghats.
Which of the factors given above influences the onset of the Indian monsoon?

so.t:
● Statement 1 is correct: : The primary driver of the Indian monsoon is the development of a low-pressure system over the Tibetan Plateau during the summer. As the land heats up, the warm air rises, creating a low-pressure zone that attracts moist winds from the Indian Ocean, bringing rainfall to the subcontinent.
● Statement 2 is incorrect: The Western Ghats do play a role in the monsoon, even though their high temperatures don't directly influence the monsoon's onset. The Western Ghats act as a barrier that intercepts the rain-laden monsoon winds that come from the southwest during late summer. This results in rainfall along the western coast. The winds that pass over the Western Ghats heat up and lose humidity, resulting in little rainfall for the Western Ghats. This area is known as a rain-shadow area. The Western Ghats play an important role in determining India's climate and seasons.

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Mackenzie river:
1. It is the longest river of Canada and the largest river system on the North American continent.
2. It is experiencing historically low water-levels due to extreme heat and minimal precipitation.
3. It empties into the North Sea in the Atlantic Ocean.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
● Statement 1 is not correct: Mackenzie River originates from Great Slave Lake. The Mackenzie River system is the second largest river system on the North American continent, after only the Mississippi-Missouri River system. It is Canada’s longest river and the second longest in North America. It flows through Canada’s Northwest Territories.
● Statement 2 is correct: The Mackenzie River is currently experiencing record-low water levels due to extreme heat and minimal precipitation. These conditions have severely impacted local communities that depend on the river for transportation and fishing.
● Statement 3 is not correct: It empties into the Beaufort Sea in the Arctic Ocean. It has been crucial for transportation and the extraction of natural resources. The River basin is home to a diverse range of wildlife, including numerous fish species, migratory birds, and mammals such as beavers and moose.

q.t:
Consider the following statements about types of pollutants:
1. Primary pollutants persist in the form in which they are added to the environment.
2. Peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN) is a type of Primary pollutant.
3. Quantitative Pollutants do not occur in nature and are man-made.
4. DDT (Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane) is classified as a quantitative pollutant.
How many of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
Pollution is defined as ‘an addition or excessive addition of certain materials to the physical environment (water, air and lands), making it less fit or unfit for life’.
● Pollutants are the materials or factors, which cause adverse effect on the natural quality of any component of the environment.
● For example, smoke from industries and automobiles, chemicals from factories, radioactive substances from nuclear plants, sewage of houses and discarded household articles are the common pollutants.
Statement 1 is correct:
● Primary pollutants persist in the form in which they are added to the environment.
● Examples: DDT, plastic.
Statement 2 is not correct:
● Secondary Pollutants are formed by interaction among the primary pollutants.
● For example, peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN) is formed by the interaction of nitrogen oxides and hydrocarbons.
Statement 3 is not correct:
● Quantitative Pollutants occur in nature and become pollutant when their concentration reaches beyond a threshold level. E.g. carbon dioxide, nitrogen oxide.
Statement 4 is not correct:
● Qualitative Pollutants do not occur in nature and are man-made.
● E.g. fungicides, herbicides, DDT etc.
Note:
● Biodegradable Pollutants: Waste products, which are degraded by microbial action. E.g. sewage.
● Non-biodegradable Pollutants: Pollutants, which are not decomposed by microbial action.
E.g. plastics, glass, DDT, salts of heavy metals, radioactive substances etc.

opt.t: Introduction of communalism into Indian politics by Lord Minto.
Extremists' lack of faith in the capacity of the moderates to negotiate with the British Government.
Aurobindo Ghosh's inability to be elected as the President of the Indian National Congress.

q.t: What was the main reason for the split in the Indian National Congress at Surat in 1907?

so.t:
There was a fundamental difference between the political ideologies of the extremists' and moderates which led to their split at Surat Session of Congress in 1907.
The veteran moderate politicians refused to accommodate the new extremist's trend within the Congress policies and
programmes, and this led to the split in the Congress in its Surat session in 1907.
The Extremists, during the Swadeshi Movement, decided to give a call for wide passive resistance and boycott of schools, colleges, legislative councils, municipalities, law courts, etc.
The Moderates, encouraged by the news that council reforms were on the anvil, decided to tone down the scheme adopted at Calcutta Session.
The Extremists thought that the people had been aroused and the time had come for direct confrontation with the
British. They considered the Moderates to be a drag on the movement and no longer have faith in the capacity of
the moderates to negotiate with the British Government. They decided that it was necessary to Part Company with
the Moderates, even if it meant a split in the Congress. On the other hand, the Moderates thought that it would be
dangerous that stage to associate with the Extremists whose anti-imperialist agitation would be ruthlessly suppressed by the mighty colonial forces.
Both sides adopted rigid positions, which ultimately led to split. The moderates after the Surat Split in 1907 demanded colonial self-government, as against the extremist demand of complete independence.

opt.t: It determines the objective for the making of necessary laws.
It enables the creation of political offices and a government.
It defines and limits the powers of government.
It secures social justice, social equality and social security.

q.t: Which one of the following statements best reflects the Chief purpose of the 'Constitution' of a country?

so.t:
Option c is correct: The Constitution contains the basic principles and laws of a nation that determine the powers
and duties of the government. A constitution serves multiple purposes. It provides a legitimate legal and political basis
for the Government to propose and enact laws, organizing public service and settling disputes.
Although the Constitution determines the objectives (DPSP) for the making of necessary laws and aims to promote social, economic and political democracy, its chief purpose is to limit the power of government. In fact the Constitutional government is by definition limited government.

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. India
2. United States of America (USA)
3. Japan
4. Australia
5. European Union (EU)
How many of the above are members of the ‘Biopharmaceutical Alliance’?

so.t:
● Biopharmaceutical Alliance is an alliance of India, South Korea, Japan, the United States of America (USA) and the European Union (EU) to put joint efforts into building a resilient supply chain in the biopharmaceutical sector. It was inaugurated in San Diego during the Bio International Convention 2024. So, points 1, 2, 3 and 5 are correct and point 4 is not correct.
● It was launched in response to the drug supply shortages experienced during the Covid-19 pandemic. The participants agreed to coordinate the respective countries' biopolicies, regulations and research and development support measures. So, only four of the above are members of the ‘Biopharmaceutical Alliance’.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

opt.t: Only I and II are correct
Only II and III are correct
Only I and III are correct

q.t:
Consider the following statements about Punjabi festivals:
Statement I: Vaisakhi marks both the harvest festival and the formation of the Khalsa.
Statement II: Lohri is celebrated to mark the end of winter and is associated with the Rabi crop harvest.
Statement III: Karva Chauth originated in Punjab and spread to other parts of North India.

so.t:
Explanation:
• Statement I: Correct. Vaisakhi marks both the harvest festival, celebrating the ripening of the Rabi crop in Punjab, and the historic formation of the Khalsa Panth by Guru Gobind Singh in 1699. It is a key Sikh festival with dual significance.
• Statement II: Correct. Lohri is celebrated to mark the end of winter and is closely associated with the harvest of the Rabi crops in Punjab. It involves bonfires and folk celebrations symbolizing warmth and prosperity.
• Statement III: Incorrect. Karva Chauth did not originate in Punjab. It is primarily a North Indian festival celebrated in states like Rajasthan, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, and Punjab has adopted the festival relatively later. It did not originate specifically in Punjab but has broader North Indian roots.

opt.t: . Statement I is correct, but Statement II is incorrect
. Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is correct
. Both statements are correct and Statement II is a logical consequence of Statement I

q.t:
Consider the following two statements about HIV-AIDS and TB co-infection:
Statement I: HIV-positive individuals have a 20-30 times higher risk of developing active tuberculosis compared to HIV-negative individuals.
Statement II: TB is the leading cause of death among HIV-positive patients worldwide.

so.t:
Explanation:
• Statement I: People who are HIV-positive have a 20-30 times higher risk of developing active tuberculosis compared to HIV-negative individuals. This is because HIV weakens the immune system, making it more difficult to contain latent TB infections and increasing susceptibility to active TB disease.
• Statement II: Tuberculosis is the leading cause of death among HIV-positive patients worldwide. It accounts for about 30% of AIDS-related deaths globally and remains a major challenge in managing HIV/AIDS patients.
• Since HIV infection drastically increases the risk of TB (Statement I), it logically follows that TB becomes a major cause of mortality in HIV patients (Statement II).

q.t: According to Morgenthau, the best means of preserving peace in a society of sovereign nations is :

so.t: According to Morgenthau, diplomacy is the best means of preserving peace as it reconciles conflicting national interests through negotiation, compromise, and prudence, preventing war in an anarchic international system. While national interest drives state actions, it often leads to conflicts rather than resolving them. Foreign trade, though promoting economic interdependence, is a liberal theory element and insufficient in addressing power struggles central to realism. Autonomy ensures sovereignty but does not prevent conflicts; rather, it can exacerbate tensions without cooperative mechanisms. Thus, diplomacy, as a practical tool for conflict resolution, is essential to maintaining peace among sovereign nations

q.t:
Which of the following features of the Indus Valley Civilization are correct?
1. A monarchical system was well established.
2. Long-distance trade was maintained with the Mesopotamian region.
3. Bricks used in construction were of a uniform size throughout the Indus Valley culture.
Select the correct statement/statements using the codes given below.

so.t:
Statement 1 is not correct:
● Archaeological records provide no immediate answers for a centre of power in Indus Valley culture.
● A large building found at Mohenjodaro was labelled as a palace by archaeologists but no spectacular finds were associated with it.
● Some archaeologists are of the opinion that Harappan society had no rulers, and that everybody enjoyed equal status.
● Others feel there was no single ruler but several, that Mohenjodaro had a separate ruler, Harappa another, and so forth.
● Yet others argue that there was a single state, given the similarity in artefacts, the evidence for planned settlements, the standardised ratio of brick size, and the establishment of settlements near sources of raw material.
● According to DD Kosambi, there might be a priest monarch, however R.S. Sharma believes there could be merchant rulers.
● It is said that there are several ideas as to what form of centralising power dominated the region, none of which has been proven beyond question.
Statement 2 is correct:
● Recent archaeological finds suggest that copper was probably brought from Oman, on the south eastern tip of the Arabian Peninsula. A distinctive type of vessel, a large Harappan jar coated with a thick layer of black clay has been found at Omani sites.
● Mesopotamian texts datable to the third millennium BCE refer to copper coming from a region called Magan, perhaps a name for Oman, and interestingly enough copper found at Mesopotamian sites also contains traces of nickel.
● Other archaeological finds suggestive of long-distance contacts include Harappan seals, weights, dice and beads. In this context, it is worth noting that Mesopotamian texts mention contact with regions named Dilmun (probably the island of Bahrain), Magan and Meluhha, possibly the Harappan region.
Statement 3 is correct:
● There are indications of complex decisions being taken and implemented in Harappan society.
● The extraordinary uniformity of Harappan artefacts as evident in pottery, seals, weights and bricks.
● Notably, bricks, though obviously not produced in any single centre, were of a uniform ratio throughout the region, from Jammu to Gujarat.
● The settlements were strategically set up in specific locations for various reasons. Besides, labour was mobilised for making bricks and for the construction of massive walls and platforms.

q.t:
What were the reasons which led to bank nationalization of 1969?

1. Expansion of bank branches was mostly in urban areas
2. Agriculture and small-scale industries were not getting proper access to banking facilities
3. Bank credit was not utilized for financing projects according to Plan priorities
4. Easy and cheap availability of credit to a few industrial houses had encouraged growth of monopolies
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
All were the reasons leading to bank nationalization. And the objectives of bank nationalization were:
 Channelizing bank capital into rural and semi urban areas
 Checking misuse of banking capital for financing speculative activities
 To shift from class banking to mass banking
 Make banks an integral part of socio-economic development

opt.t: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II explains Statement-I
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct, but Statement-II does not explain Statement-I
Statement-I is correct, but Statement-II is incorrect
Statement-I is incorrect, but Statement-II is correct

q.t:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I: The Indian climate is characterized by distinct seasons, including the southwest monsoon, winter, and
summer.
Statement II: The variability in the Indian climate is primarily due to the seasonal shifts in the monsoon winds and the influence of the Himalayan range.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

so.t:
● Statement-I is correct: The Indian climate is indeed characterized by distinct seasons - summer, southwest monsoon, and winter. These seasons are the result of the shifting atmospheric pressure patterns, the movement of the sun, and the seasonal reversal of winds.
● Statement-Ii is incorrect: While the seasonal shifts in the monsoon winds do cause variability in the Indian climate, the primary cause of variability is the monsoon winds themselves, not the Himalayan range. The Himalayan range plays a significant role in modifying temperature and weather patterns, but it does not primarily drive the variability in the Indian climate. The main cause of climate variability is the monsoon system, which brings heavy rainfall during the monsoon months and dry conditions during the winter.

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to Death Valley:
1. It is a rift valley in the USA formed due to the sinking of a huge expanse of rock between major block-faulted mountains.
2. It holds the record for the highest temperature ever recorded on Earth.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
● Statement 1 is correct: The Death Valley is a desert valley located in the southeastern part of California, United States. It lies in the Great Basin to the east of the Sierra Nevada Mountains, in the northern part of the Mojave Desert. It is a graben, or rift valley, formed by the sinking of a tremendous expanse of rock lying between parallel uplifted, tilted-block mountain ranges to the east and west.
● Statement 2 is correct: Death Valley holds the record for the highest temperature ever recorded on Earth (56.7 degree Celsius recorded more than 100 years ago in 1913). Badwater Basin, an endorheic basin, in Death Valley is the lowest point in North America.

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. Stratospheric ozone
2. Unstable air condition
3. Colder months with minimum sunlight
Which of the above conditions are relevant to the formation of ‘Photochemical smog’?

so.t:
Option (d) is correct answer:
● Photochemical smog is a mixture of pollutants that are formed when nitrogen oxides and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) react to sunlight, creating a brown haze above cities. It tends to occur more often in summer, because that is when we have the most sunlight.
● Primary pollutants: The two major primary pollutants, nitrogen oxides and VOCs, combine to change in sunlight in a series of chemical reactions, to create what are known as secondary pollutants.
● Secondary pollutants: The secondary pollutant that causes the most concern is the ozone that forms at ground level. While ozone in the stratosphere protects earth from harmful UV radiation, ozone on the ground is hazardous to human health. Ground-level ozone is formed when vehicle emissions containing nitrogen oxides (primarily from vehicle exhaust) and volatile organic compounds (from paints, solvents, printing inks, petroleum products, vehicles, etc.) interact in the presence of sunlight. Many other hazardous substances are also formed, such as peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN).
● How location and weather can have an effect?
o The topography of the area surrounding a city can vastly influence the formation of photochemical smog. Because of the restriction of air movement, a city in a valley can experience problems that a city on an open plain may not.
o Normally the layer of air closest to the earth’s surface is warmer than the air higher in the atmosphere because the heat of the sun is re-radiated (warmed by the earth’s surface).
o The higher level cool air sinks and is then warmed and displaced upwards in a convection cycle. This condition is called ‘unstable’ and helps to carry pollutants upwards, where they are dispersed and diluted. This cycle is usually assisted by higher wind speeds.
o However, when the opposite occurs—a temperature inversion—cities can experience prolonged periods of photochemical smog.
o An inversion is formed when a ceiling of warmer air traps the cooler layer of air, which contains the pollutants, near the ground’s surface. This hinders the ability of the pollutants to rise to the atmosphere and be dispersed.
o After an inversion has formed, it keeps any smog that is present close to the ground, maximising its detrimental effect. This ‘stable’ condition may last for several days.
o Higher sunlight intensity during summer months lead to more photochemical reactions.
Additional information: How is smog formed?
● Nitrogen dioxide (NO2) can be broken down by sunlight to form nitric oxide (NO) and an oxygen radical (O).
● Oxygen radicals can then react with atmospheric oxygen (O2) to form ozone (O3).
● O3 Ozone is consumed by nitric oxide to produce nitrogen dioxide and oxygen.
● Harmful products, such as PAN, are produced by reactions of nitrogen dioxide with various hydrocarbons (R), which are compounds made from carbon, hydrogen and other substances.
● The main source of these hydrocarbons is the VOCs.
● Similarly, oxygenated organic and inorganic compounds (ROx) react with nitric oxide to produce more nitrogen oxides.
● The significance of the presence of the VOCs in these last two reactions is paramount.
● Ozone is normally consumed by nitric oxide. However, when VOCs are present, nitric oxide and nitrogen dioxide are consumed in reactions, allowing the build up of ground level ozone.

q.t:
Consider the following statements
1. The first woman President of the Indian National Congress was Sarojini Naidu.
2. The first Muslim President of the Indian National Congress was Badruddin Tyabji.
Which of the statements given above is / are correct?

so.t:
Statement 1 is incorrect. Annie Besant who was instrumental in creation of Home Rule League presided over the 1917 session in Calcutta and became the first woman president of INC.
Sarojini Naidu joined the Indian National Movement in the wake of the 1905 partition of Bengal. She was elected as the President of the Indian National Congress Party in 1925, Kanpur Session. Thus, Sarnjini Naidu was the first Indian women President of INC.
Statement 2 is correct. Badruddin Tyabji came back to India from England in December 1867 and became the first Indian barrister in the High Court of Bombay.
Badruddin Tyabji became the first Muslim president of the Indian National Congress. Overall, he was the third President of the Congress after WC Bannerjee and Dadabhai Naoroji (1886).

q.t:
Match list-I with list-II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists:
List-I (Schedule)
 List-II (Subject)
A. Third Schedule
 2. Forms of Oath or Affirmations
B. Fourth Schedule
 1. Allocation of seats in the Council of States
C. Seventh Schedule
 4. List of subject matter of laws to be made by the Parliament and by State Legislatures
D. Eighth Schedule
 3. Languages
Code:

so.t:
Exp) Option d is the correct answer.
A. Third Schedule- (2) This schedule contains the text of the oath or affirmation that is to be taken by the
President, Vice-President, Governors, Speakers and Deputy Speakers of Legislatures, and Judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts.
B. Fourth Schedule- (1) This schedule is associated with the allocation of seats in the Rajya Sabha to the states
and the union territories.
C. Seventh Schedule- (4) The Union List (98 subjects), the State List (59 subjects) and the Concurrent List (52 subjects).
D. Eighth Schedule- (3) This schedule is specifically related to Languages. Altogether there are 22 scheduled languages in India.
Important Tips
Brief overview of the Schedules of the Indian Constitution:
1. Schedule 1: The States and the Union Territories of India.
2. Schedule 2: Provisions related to emoluments, allowances, privileges and so on as to the President, Vice-President, Governors, Speakers and Deputy Speakers of Legislatures and Judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts.
3. Schedule 5: The Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes.
4. Schedule 6: Administration of Tribal Areas in the States of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram.
5. Schedule 9: It contains a list of central and state laws shielded from judicial scrutiny, added by the Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951, to protect agrarian reform and abolishing the Zamindari system
6. Schedule 10: Disqualification on Ground of Defections (Added via 52ª4 Act, 1985).
7. Schedule 11: Panchayats (Added via 73rd Act, 1992).
8. Schedule 12: Municipalities (Added via 74' Act, 1992).

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition (SIGHT):
1. It provides financial incentives to support domestic manufacturing of electrolysers.
2. It is implemented by the Indian Renewable Energy Development Agency.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
● The Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition (SIGHT) Programme is a major financial measure with an outlay of ₹ 17,490 crore. It consists of two distinct financial incentive mechanisms:
○ to support domestic manufacturing of electrolysers. So, statement 1 is correct.
○ production of Green Hydrogen
● The guidelines and incentives to promote the procurement of green hydrogen are part of the National Green
Hydrogen Mission.
● Solar Energy Corporation of India is the implementing agency for this Scheme. Recently, the SECI has issued
a Request for Selection (RfS) for Selection of Green Ammonia Producers for the production of Green Ammonia
in India under Mode 2A of the SIGHT Programme of the National Green Hydrogen Mission. So, statement 2
is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t: Which of the following rivers does NOT flow through Punjab but significantly affects its water ?

so.t:
Yamuna's Course:
• Origin: Yamunotri glacier in Uttarakhand
• Path: Flows through Uttarakhand → Himachal Pradesh → Haryana → Delhi → UP → finally joins Ganges at Allahabad
• Punjab connection: Never enters Punjab territory
Distance from Punjab:
• Yamuna flows east of Punjab
• Closest point is still in Haryana territory
• No direct geographical connection with Punjab
How Yamuna Affects Punjab's Water Resources
The Sutlej-Yamuna Link (SYL) Canal Saga:
1. Historical Background (1960s-1970s):
• Punjab Reorganization Act 1966: When Haryana was carved out of Punjab
• Water sharing agreement: Punjab agreed to share river waters with newly formed Haryana
• Original plan: Build a canal linking surplus Sutlej water to Yamuna basin for Haryana
2. The Canal Project:
• Purpose: Transfer Punjab's "surplus" Sutlej water to Haryana via Yamuna system
• Length: Approximately 214 km
• Capacity: Designed to carry about 7.2 MAF (Million Acre Feet) annually
• Route: From Punjab → Haryana → connect with Yamuna tributaries
3. The Dispute Timeline:
1970s-1980s:
• Construction began: Work started on Punjab side
• Political opposition: Growing resistance in Punjab
• Completion: Punjab side largely completed, Haryana side incomplete
1990s-2000s:
• Punjab's withdrawal: Punjab stopped work citing water scarcity
• Legal battles: Multiple Supreme Court cases
• Deadlock: Project remained incomplete
2010s-Present:
• Supreme Court interventions: Multiple orders to complete the canal
• Punjab's resistance: Continues to refuse, citing water crisis
• Haryana's pressure: Demands completion for agricultural needs
4. Current Status:
• Incomplete project: Canal remains unfinished after 50+ years
• Legal limbo: Ongoing Supreme Court proceedings
• Political issue: Major source of Punjab-Haryana tension
• Cost escalation: Project costs multiplied from original estimates
Why This Affects Punjab's Water Resources
Legal Obligations:
• Supreme Court orders: Punjab legally bound to provide water
• Inter-state agreements: Binding commitments under various accords
• Constitutional duty: Article 262 mandates resolution of water disputes
Practical Implications:
• Water allocation: Punjab must reserve water for potential SYL requirements
• Agricultural planning: Affects crop planning and irrigation strategies
• Political capital: Consumes enormous political and legal resources
• Economic burden: Legal costs and incomplete infrastructure

opt.t: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is correct
Both statements are correct but represent independent scientific principles

q.t:
Consider the following statements about practical applications in everyday life:
Statement I: UV-C light (200-280 nm wavelength) is effective for disinfection because it damages the DNA/RNA of microorganisms, preventing their reproduction.
Statement II: LED lights consume less electricity than incandescent bulbs because they convert electrical energy more efficiently into light energy rather than heat energy.

so.t:
• Statement I: UV-C light with wavelength in the range of 200-280 nm is effective for disinfection because it damages the DNA and RNA of microorganisms. This damage, particularly through the formation of pyrimidine dimers (e.g., thymine dimers), prevents replication and leads to the death of bacteria, viruses, and other pathogens.
• Statement II: LED lights consume less electricity than incandescent bulbs because they are much more efficient at converting electrical energy into light rather than heat. Incandescent bulbs waste most of their energy producing heat, while LEDs produce very little heat and thus consume far less power for the same amount of visible light output.
• These two statements describe different physical and biological phenomena — one about germicidal ultraviolet radiation and another about light production efficiency in LED lighting — so they are correct but independent.

q.t: Who believes that a theory of International politics is “but a specific instance of a general theory of politics?

so.t:
Hans J. Morgenthau, a prominent realist, argued that the principles of political realism, which underpin the theory of international politics, are derived from a general theory of politics. He believed that the struggle for power and interest is a fundamental aspect of politics at all levels—domestic and international. His emphasis on the universality of power politics reflects his view that international politics is simply one application of these broader principles.
Tucker: Not associated with this statement; focuses more on critiques of realism. Kennan: Known for the "containment policy," not for a general theory of politics. Wasserman: Does not relate to this theoretical framework. Thus, Morgenthau’s perspective aligns with the statement

opt.t: A place where kings conducted ‘Rajasuya’ sacrifices.
A dwelling place for Buddhist monks.
A place for philosophical debates among thinkers of different sects.
A centre for Vedic education for Brahmins.

q.t: With reference to the history of ancient India, the place ‘Kutagarashala’ was associated with:

so.t:
Option (c) is the correct answer:
● Many ideas found in the Upanishads (c. sixth century BCE onwards) show that people were curious about the meaning of life, the possibility of life after death, and rebirth.
● Such issues were hotly debated. Thinkers were concerned with understanding and expressing the nature of the ultimate reality.
● And others, outside the Vedic tradition, asked whether or not there even was a single ultimate reality. People also began speculating on the significance of the sacrificial tradition.
● A glimpse of lively discussions and debates from Buddhist texts mention that as many as 64 sects or schools of thought were there.
● Teachers travelled from place to place, trying to convince one another as well as laypersons, about the validity of their philosophy or the way they understood the world.
● Debates took place in the kutagarashala-literally, a hut with a pointed roof-or in groves where travelling mendicants halted. If a philosopher succeeded in convincing one of his rivals, the followers of the latter also became his disciples. ● So support for any particular sect could grow and shrink over time. Many of these teachers, including Mahavira and the Buddha, questioned the authority of the Vedas.
● They also emphasised individual agency-suggesting that men and women could strive to attain liberation from the trials and tribulations of worldly existence.
● This was in marked contrast to the Brahmanical position, wherein, as we have seen, an individual’s existence was thought to be determined by his or her birth in a specific caste or gender.

opt.t: India’s exports will be more competitive than US
India’s exports will be less competitive than US

q.t: If rupee is appreciating in real terms with respect to US dollars, then:

so.t: When a currency appreciates either in nominal or real terms then export competitiveness decreases. If rupee is appreciating that means India’s export competitiveness is decreasing but you cannot say India’s exports is less competitive.

q.t:
El Niño refers to the abnormal warming of sea surface temperatures in the eastern tropical Pacific Ocean. Which of the following are its potential impacts?
1. Weakens monsoon rainfall over the Indian subcontinent.
2. Enhances cyclone formation in the Bay of Bengal.
3. Reduces cyclone activity in the Arabian Sea.
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:

so.t:
· Statement 1 is correct: El Niño weakens the Indian monsoon due to its impact on atmospheric circulation, reducing the strength of monsoon winds and moisture transport.
· Statement 2 is incorrect: El Niño typically suppresses cyclonic activity in the Bay of Bengal due to unfavorable wind patterns and atmospheric conditions.
· Statement 3 is correct: El Niño is associated with weakened cyclonic activity in the Arabian Sea due to a decline in sea surface temperature differences and disrupted wind patterns.

q.t:
Consider the following pairs:
S.NO.
 Air Pollutant
 Primary Source
1.
 Radon
 Mainly from carpets and insulation foam
2.
 Formaldehyde
 Emitted naturally by the soil
3.
 Hydrocarbons
 Burning of fossil fuels
Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?

so.t:
Pollutants in indoor and outdoor environments can have a range of health effects.
● Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) are emitted from products such as perfumes, hair sprays, furniture polish, glues, air fresheners, and wood preservatives. Short-term exposure can cause irritation to the eyes, nose, and throat, headaches, nausea, and loss of coordination, while long-term exposure is suspected to damage the liver and other parts of the body.
● Tobacco smoke generates harmful chemicals and is a known carcinogen. It can cause immediate symptoms like burning eyes and throat irritation, and over time, lead to severe health issues such as cancer, bronchitis, asthma, and a decrease in lung function.
Pair 1 is not correctly matched:
● Radon, a naturally occurring gas released by the soil, is often trapped in poorly ventilated modern homes, increasing the risk of lung cancer.
Pair 2 is not correctly matched:
● Formaldehyde, found in carpets, particle boards, and insulation foam, is an indoor pollutant that causes eye and nose irritation and allergic reactions.
Pair 3 is correctly matched:
● Hydrocarbons released by the burning of fossil fuels have serious health effects, including carcinogenic impacts on the lungs, kidney damage, hypertension, respiratory distress, irritation of the eyes, nose, and throat, and conditions like asthma and bronchitis.
● These pollutants collectively pose significant health risks, particularly in poorly ventilated
spaces.

q.t:
Match list-I with Hst-U and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists:
List-I
 List-II
A. Chhittagaon Armoury Raid
 1. Kalpana Dutt
B. Abhinav Bharat
 2. Guru Ram Singh
C. Anushilan Samiti
 3. Vinayak Damodar Savarkar
D. Kuka Movement
 4. Aurobindo Ghosh
Code:

so.t:
Kalpana Dutt was a remarkable Indian revolutionary who participated in the Chittagong Armory Raid led by Surya Sen on 18 April 1930. In the jail, she was visited by Gandhi and received letters from Rabindranath Tagore and C F Andrews. The latter addressed her as his daughter from his previous life. Kalpana was given a life sentence but was eventually released in 1939.
Baba Ram Singh Kuka started the Kuka Movement in 1872. The Kukas were also referred Namdharis, who were a sect within Sikhism. They started as a group for religious purification in Sikhism under Ram Singh. He was the first Indian who used Non-Cooperation as an ideology and boycotted British services and goods. The movement received a political overtone with the established aim of restoring Sikh rule in Punjab and ousting the foreign powers.
Vinayak Damodar Savarkar was the founder of theAbhinav Bharat society. It was one among several such revolutionary societies functioning in Maharashtra at that time, which believed in the overthrow of British rule through armed rebellion.
Aurobindo Ghosh was closely associated with the Anushilan Samiti. Anushilan Samiti was a well-known covert revolutionary organisation that operated out of Bengal in the 20th century with the goal of removing colonial authority.

q.t: . The Citizenship (Amendment) Act falls under which one of the following parts of the Constitution of India?

so.t:
Article 11 of the Indian Constitution grants the Parliament the authority to regulate citizenship rights, and it is under this provision that the Citizenship (Amendment) Act (CAA) was enacted. As Article 11 is part of Part II of the Constitution of India, the CAA can be classified within the framework of Part II of the constitution.
Important Tip
. The Citizenship Act, 1955 was amended 6 times in 1986, 1992, 2003, 2005, 2015 and 2019.
. The CAA 2019 provides citizenship on the basis of religion to six undocumented non-Muslim communities (Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Parsis and Christians) from Pakistan, Afghanistan and Bangladesh who entered India on or before 31st December, 2014.
. It exempts the members of the six communities from any criminal case under the Foreigners Act, 1946 and the Passport Act, 1920.

q.t:
Which of the following statements with regard to the Preamble to the Constitution of India is/are correct?
1. Equality of status and equality of opportunity find mention in the Preamble.
2. The expression 'unity and integrity of the Nation' was not there in the Preamble since the beginning.
Select the correct answer using the code given below :-

so.t:
Statement 1 is correct- Equality entails the absence of privileges or discrimination within society, and the
Preamble emphasizes equality of status and opportunity for all citizens.
Statement 2 is correct-Through the 42nd Amendment Act of 1976, the phrase was added to the Preamble to emphasize
the importance of maintaining the unity and integrity of India as a sovereign, socialist, secular, and democratic
republic.
Important Tips
The Indian Constitution's Preamble is derived from the US Constitution and is often referred to as the "identity
card" of the Constitution.
It is based on the Objective Resolution proposed by Pandit Nehru and was amended once through the 42nd
Constitutional Amendment Act, 1972, to include the words "Socialist, Secular and Integrity."
The Preamble is non-justiciable and does not grant legislative power or restrict it. It is an integral part of the Constitution and can be amended by a simple majority under Article 368. It was added by the Constituent Assembly after the enactment of the rest of the Constitution.

q.t:
Consider the following information:
Report
 Released by
 Key Finding
1. Child Nutrition Report
 United Nations International Children's Emergency Fund
 50% of children under age 5 are experiencing severe food poverty
2. Global Gender Gap Report
 World Economic Forum
 Global Gender Gap 2024 score showed an improvement than last year
3. A World of Debt Report
 World Bank
 Half of global public debt is owed by developing countries
In which of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?

so.t:
● The Child Nutrition Report 2024 was released by the United Nations International Children's Emergency Fund (UNICEF). UNICEF defines child food poverty as children’s inability to access and consume a nutritious and diverse diet in early childhood (first five years of life). Highlights of the 2024 Report:
○ One in four children under age 5 around the world (some 27% of 181 million) is experiencing severe food poverty and is vulnerable to life-threatening malnutrition. So, row 1 is not correctly matched.
○ Over two-thirds of the 181 million young children living in severe food poverty live in South Asia and sub Saharan Africa.
○ About half (97 million) of the children in severe food poverty live in middle and upper-income households.
● Global Gender Gap Report is an annual index designed to measure gender equality. It benchmarks gender based gaps in four areas: economic participation and opportunity, educational attainment, health and survival; and political empowerment. It is released by the World Economic Forum (WEF). Highlights of 2024 Report:
○ The global gender gap score in 2024 for all 146 countries stands at 68.5%, a 0.1 percentage point improvement on last year. So, row 2 is correctly matched.
○ India slipped two places to 129th place as Iceland retained its top position in the rankings for the 15th consecutive year.
○ India closed 64.1% of its gender gap in 2024.
○ In South Asia, India was ranked fifth after Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and Bhutan, while Pakistan was ranked last.
● A World of Debt Report 2024 was released by United Nations Trade and Development (UNCTAD).
Highlights of 2024 Report:
○ Almost a third of global public debt is owed by developing countries. So, row 3 is not correctly matched.
○ Borrowing costs of developing countries are higher than those of developed ones.
○ A growing number of countries spend more on interest than development.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

opt.t: Both statements are true, and Statement II is the correct explanation of Statement I
Both statements are true, but Statement II is not the correct explanation of Statement I
Statement I is true, but Statement II is false

q.t:
Statement I: The concept of 'Panj Kakkars' (Five Ks) was introduced by Guru Gobind Singh as identity markers for Sikhs.
Statement II: These symbols were meant to create a distinct martial identity and promote equality among Sikhs.

so.t:
• Statement I: The concept of Panj Kakkars (Five Ks) was indeed introduced by Guru Gobind Singh in 1699 during the formation of the Khalsa. These five articles of faith are: • Kesh (uncut hair)
• Kangha (wooden comb)
• Kara (iron bracelet)
• Kachera (cotton undergarments)
• Kirpan (a small sword)
• They serve as external identity markers for Sikhs.

• Statement II: The Five Ks were meant to create a distinct martial identity for Sikhs, emphasizing courage and readiness to defend righteousness. They also promote equality among all Sikhs by giving all baptized members the same symbols regardless of caste, social status, or gender. This was a revolutionary move toward social equality.
Both statements are true, and the purpose given in Statement II correctly explains why the Five Ks were introduced by Guru Gobind Singh, hence making option A correct.

opt.t: Low temperature alone prevents bacterial growth in vegetables
High humidity prevents water loss from vegetables, maintaining cellular integrity and reducing entry points for pathogens
Crisper drawers have UV sterilization that kills bacteria
High humidity increases the vitamin content of vegetables

q.t: A housewife notices that vegetables stored in her refrigerator's crisper drawer (high humidity zone) stay fresh longer than those stored in regular compartments. This is primarily because:

so.t:
• Option A is incomplete. While low temperature slows bacterial growth, it doesn't explain why crisper drawers are more effective than other refrigerator compartments at the same temperature.
• Option B is correct. Vegetables lose freshness primarily through dehydration (water loss through transpiration). High humidity in crisper drawers reduces this water loss, maintaining the vegetables' cellular structure. When cells remain turgid and intact, they provide better barrier protection against pathogenic microorganisms. Wilted vegetables have compromised cell walls that allow easier pathogen entry.
• Option C is incorrect. Household refrigerator crisper drawers don't contain UV sterilization systems.
• Option D is incorrect. Humidity doesn't increase vitamin content; it only preserves existing nutrients by maintaining cellular integrity.
• This demonstrates the practical application of plant physiology and food microbiology in daily food storage practices.

q.t: ------ was the cradle of Marxist-Leninist theory of international relations.

so.t:
The Soviet Union was the cradle of Marxist-Leninist theory of international relations. This theory, rooted in the ideas of Karl Marx and further developed by Vladimir Lenin, emerged as a critique of capitalism and imperialism, emphasizing class struggle and the role of economic structures in shaping global relations. The Soviet Union institutionalized and propagated Marxist-Leninist thought as the ideological foundation of its foreign policy, particularly through its focus on anti-imperialism and the promotion of socialist revolutions worldwide
China: While China later adapted Marxism-Leninism to its context, it was not the origin of this theory
Japan: Not associated with Marxism-Leninism in international relations
America: A capitalist democracy, it opposed Marxist-Leninist ideology

q.t:
Regarding the ‘Tripitaka’ of Buddhism, which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. ‘Sutta Pitaka’ included rules and regulations for those who joined the sangha.
2. The teachings of Buddha were included in the ‘Vinaya Pitaka’.
3. ‘Abhidhamma Pitaka’ contains commentaries on Buddhist doctrines.
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:

so.t:
The Buddha (and other teachers) taught orally-through discussion and debate.
● Men and women (perhaps children as well) attended these discourses and discussed what they heard.
● None of the Buddha’s speeches were written down during his lifetime.
● After his death (c. fifth-fourth century BCE) his teachings were compiled by his disciples at a council of “elders” or senior monks at Vesali (Pali for Vaishali in present-day Bihar).
● These compilations were known as Tipitaka -literally, three baskets to hold different types of texts.
● They were first transmitted orally and then written and classified according to length as well as subject matter.
Statement 1 is not correct:
● The Vinaya Pitaka included rules and regulations for those who joined the sangha or monastic order.
Statement 2 is not correct:
● Buddha’s teachings were included in the Sutta Pitaka.
Statement 3 is correct:
● The Abhidhamma Pitaka dealt with philosophical matters. It contains explanations and commentaries on Buddhist doctrines
● Each pitaka comprised a number of individual texts.
Additional Information:
● As Buddhism travelled to new regions such as Sri Lanka, other texts such as the Dipavamsa (literally, the chronicle of the island) and Mahavamsa (the great chronicle) were written, containing regional histories of Buddhism.
● Many of these works contained biographies of the Buddha. Some of the oldest texts are in Pali, while later compositions are in Sanskrit.
● When Buddhism spread to East Asia, pilgrims such as Fa Xian and Xuan Zang travelled all the way from China to India in search of texts.
● These they took back to their own country, where they were translated by scholars.

q.t:
Suppose due to higher domestic inflation, rupee started depreciating but RBI used the Forex reserves to
prevent the depreciation of Rupee. Then consider the following statements:
1. REER will increase
2. REER will decrease
3. Exports will become more competitive
4. No impact on trade competitiveness
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
When rupee appreciates (either in nominal or real terms) that means it is becoming more valuable then its competitiveness decreases. For example, when potato price is increasing from Rs. 10/kg to Rs. 15/kg that means value of potato is increasing/appreciating and potato is becoming less competitive. When something becomes costlier/appreciates then it becomes less competitive.
Second thing is, when rupee appreciates in nominal terms or real terms then our NER/NEER or RER/REER increases. For example, if "$1 = Rs. 70" then it can be written as $.014/Rs. (abroad w.r.t. domestic is the standard) and if this number .014 is increasing that means we are getting more dollars per rupees and rupee is appreciating.
So, when NEER and REER increases then rupee appreciates in nominal and real terms and rupee becomes less competitive.
Explanation:
Real exchange rate (RER) = (NER* Domestic price)/Abroad Price.
If domestic price (i.e. inflation) is increasing then NER will depreciate (decrease in this formula), so the increase and decrease in the numerator will cancel out and there may not be any impact on trade competitiveness. BUT if there is domestic price increase (inflation) but RBI does not allow NER to depreciating (by selling dollars in Forex) then RER (or REER) will increase.... i.e. Rupee will appreciate and our trade competitiveness will decrease. Hence, only 1st Statement is true.

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. Latitude
2. The Himalayas
3. Altitude
4. Distance from the sea
5. Proximity to the Equator
Which of the factors given above are important in determining India's climate?

so.t:
● Latitude is the correct factor: Latitude plays a significant role in shaping India’s climate. India lies between the Tropic of Cancer and the Equator, which results in a tropical climate with regional variations. The southern part of India, closer to the Equator, experiences higher temperatures year-round.
● The Himalayas is the correct factor: The Himalayas have a major impact on India’s climate. They block cold northern winds, helping maintain warmer temperatures in northern India during winter. The mountains also influence the monsoon winds, leading to heavy rainfall in the southern and eastern regions while causing a rainshadow effect in the western parts.
● Altitude is an incorrect factor: Altitude affects climate, but it is not as influential on India's overall climate as the other factors listed. Higher altitudes experience cooler temperatures, but India’s climate is primarily determined by the combination of latitude, the Himalayas, and distance from the sea.
● Distance from the sea is the correct factor: The distance from the sea is a major factor in India’s climate. Coastal areas, like those in Kerala and Mumbai, experience a more moderate climate ,with less temperature variation due to the ocean’s moderating effect. Inland areas, such as Rajasthan, experience more extreme temperature variations.
● Proximity to the Equator is an incorrect factor: While India’s latitude near the Equator influences its tropical nature, the specific "proximity to the Equator" is not a separate determining factor beyond latitude. Latitude already accounts for this aspect in determining temperature and seasonal variations.

opt.t: None of the above statements is correct

q.t:
With reference to the planet Earth, consider the following statements:
I. Rain forests produce more oxygen than that produced by oceans.
II. Marine phytoplankton and photosynthetic bacteria produce about 50% of world's oxygen.
III. Well-oxygenated surface water contains several folds higher oxygen than that in atmospheric air.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
Statement I: Rainforests do not produce more oxygen than oceans. Oceans contribute about 50% or more of the world's oxygen through marine phytoplankton and photosynthetic bacteria, while rainforests contribute significantly but less than oceans.
Statement II: Marine phytoplankton and photosynthetic bacteria produce about 50% of the world’s oxygen, making this statement correct.
Statement III: Well-oxygenated surface water does not contain several folds higher oxygen than atmospheric air. Dissolved oxygen in water is much lower in concentration compared to oxygen in the atmosphere. Surface water oxygen concentration is near equilibrium with atmospheric oxygen but not significantly higher.
Hence, only Statement II is correct.

q.t: Who among the following is associated with 'Songs from Prison', a translation of ancient Indian religious lyrics in English?

so.t: Mahatma Gandhi was associated with "Songs from Prison", a translation of ancient Indian religious lyrics from Upnishads and other religious scripture, during his imprisionment in Yarvada Jail in 1930s.

q.t:
Which of the following statements with regard to citizenship provisions of the Constitution of India is/are correct?
1. No person shall be a citizen of India by virtue of Article 5, or be deemed to be a citizen of India by virtue of Article 6 or Article 8, if he/she has voluntarily acquired the citizenship of any foreign state.
2. The Parliament has power to make any provision with respect to the acquisition and termination of citizenship and all other matters relating to citizenship.
Select the correct answer using the codes given below :

so.t:
Exp: Option c is the correct answer.
• As per article 9 No person shall be a citizen of India by virtue of article 5, or be deemed to be a citizen of India by virtue of article 6 or article 8, if he has voluntarily acquired the citizenship of any foreign State.
As per article 11 The Parliament has the right to make any provision concerning the acquisition and termination of citizenship and any other matter relating to citizenship.

q.t: It is a man-made reservoir situated in the Jhajha forest range. Its catchment areas feature dry deciduous forests surrounded by hillocks. Notably, the site hosts one of the largest congregations of bar-headed goose (Anser indicus) on the Indo Gangetic plain. It is a wetland of international importance under the Ramsar Convention. It is:

so.t:
● Nagi Bird Sanctuary was created following the construction of a dam across the Nagi River. It is a man made reservoir situated in the Jhajha forest range of Jamui district of Bihar. The catchment area, largely dry deciduous forest, is surrounded by hills.
● Due to its importance for migratory bird species, the site was recognized internationally as an Important Bird and Biodiversity Area (IBA) by BirdLife International. Notably, the site hosts one of the largest congregations of bar-headed geese (Anser indicus) on the Indo-Gangetic plain.
● It was declared as Wetlands of International importance under the Ramsar Convention on the occasion
of World Environment Day on June 5, 2024.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

opt.t: Only I and II are correct
Only II and III are correct
Only I and III are correct

q.t:
Consider the following statements about Punjab's agricultural zones:
Statement I: The cotton-wheat zone is predominantly in the Malwa region of Punjab.
Statement II: The rice-wheat cropping pattern is most intensive in the central plains of Punjab. Statement III: The Kandi belt is suitable for horticultural crops and less water-intensive farming.

so.t:
• Statement I: The cotton-wheat zone is predominantly in the Malwa region of Punjab. Malwa, characterized by black soil and semi-arid climate, is well-suited for cotton cultivation, followed by wheat. Districts like Bathinda, Mansa, and Muktsar form the core cotton belt of Punjab.
• Statement II: The rice-wheat cropping pattern is most intensive in the central plains of Punjab, where well-developed irrigation systems support this crop rotation. Rice is grown in the kharif (monsoon) season and wheat in the rabi (winter) season. This system dominates Punjab's agriculture.
• Statement III: The Kandi belt (sub-mountainous areas near the Shiwaliks) is suitable for horticultural crops and less water-intensive farming due to its undulating topography and lower water availability. This area sees cultivation of fruits, vegetables, and other less water-demanding crops, supported by emerging irrigation and water management techniques.
Hence, all three statements about Punjab's agricultural zones are accurate.

opt.t: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is correct
Both statements are correct and MCBs are indeed better for motor protection

q.t:
An electrician advises a Punjab farmer to install an MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker) instead of traditional fuses in his tube-well motor connection. Consider the following statements:
Statement I: MCBs can be reset after tripping, while fuses need replacement, making MCBs more economical for motor applications that face frequent power fluctuations.
Statement II: MCBs respond faster to overload conditions than fuses, providing better protection to expensive motor equipment.

so.t:
Explanation:
• Statement I: MCBs can be reset after tripping, unlike traditional fuses which need to be replaced once blown. This makes MCBs more economical for motor applications where power fluctuations cause frequent tripping or overloading.
• Statement II: MCBs respond faster to overload conditions than fuses, providing better and quicker protection to expensive motor equipment by minimizing damage risk. Fuses generally act slower and once they melt, the entire device must be replaced, causing downtime.
Thus, both statements are accurate and MCBs offer superior and convenient protection for tube-well motors and similar applications.

q.t: Realist approach focusses attention on the units which it views as principal actors in international politics. These units are :

so.t:
The realist approach in international relations emphasizes the centrality of states as the principal actors in international politics. Realists view the international system as anarchic, where states, driven by their national interests (primarily power and security), operate as rational and sovereign entities. This state-centric perspective underpins classical realism (e.g., Hans Morgenthau) and structural realism (e.g., Kenneth Waltz).
MNCs: Multinational corporations play a role in liberal or globalist theories, not central to realism.
Leaders: Realists focus on state behavior rather than individual leaders
Organisations: International organizations are secondary to state sovereignty in realism

q.t:
Consider the following statements related to Scheduled Banks in India:
1. Cooperative banks can also be classified as Scheduled Banks.
2. It is mandatory for a Scheduled Bank to have government ownership.
3. Scheduled Banks are included in the First Schedule of the RBI Act, 1934.
Which of the statements given above is/are not correct?

so.t:
Statement 1 is correct -
According to the RBI act, 1934, a scheduled Bank is one which is
○ Included in the second schedule of the RBI Act - Statement 3 is not correct.
○ Has a paid-up capital of not less than 5 lakhs.
○ Satisfies that its affairs are not being conducted in a manner which is detrimental to the interests of the depositors.
● It includes different categories such as Scheduled commercial Banks, Public Sector Banks, Cooperative Banks, Regional Rural Banks etc.
Statement 2 is incorrect - There is no requirement for the Government of India to hold a majority stake in Scheduled Banks. Many private sector banks are also classified as Scheduled Banks such as Axis bank, HDFC bank etc.

q.t:
Match the following winds with their characteristics:
Winds
Characteristics
1. kalbaishakhi
 Rain showers that occur in the coastal regions, especially near Mumbai.
2. Mango showers
 A type of rain that helps the flowering of mango trees in South India.
3. Blossom showers
 Pre-monsoon showers bring relief to the temperature in Bengal and surrounding areas.
4. Elephanta
 A wind that blows from the southwest and brings early monsoon rains to the west coast of India.
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?

so.t:
● Pair 1 is incorrectly matched: Norwester is a type of pre-monsoon storm that brings rain and relief to Bengal and surrounding areas. It is also known as a "kalbaishakhi" in Bengal, and it is associated with high temperatures and intense thunderstorms.
● Pair 2 is correctly matched: Mango showers are the early rains that help in the flowering of mango trees in South India. They are also important for the preparation of the monsoon season and typically occur in April-May.
● Pair 3 is incorrectly matched: Blossom showers occur on the western coast of India, particularly around Mumbai, and help in the flowering of trees in the region. They are light rains that occur before the full onset of the monsoon.
● Pair 4 is correctly matched: Elephanta refers to the early monsoon winds that blow from the southwest, bringing the first rains to the west coast of India, including Mumbai and Gujarat.

opt.t: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I
Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

q.t:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I: Article 6 of the Paris Agreement on climate change is frequently discussed in global discussions on sustainable development and climate change.
Statement II: Article 6 of the Paris Agreement on climate change sets out the principles of carbon markets.
Statement III: Article 6 of the Paris Agreement on climate change intends to promote inter-country non-market strategies to reach their climate targets.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

so.t:
Explanation:
Statement I: Article 6 of the Paris Agreement is indeed frequently discussed in global forums on sustainable development and climate change because it negotiates mechanisms of international cooperation for emissions reduction.
Statement II: Article 6 sets out the principles of carbon markets. It establishes frameworks for market-based approaches such as internationally transferred mitigation outcomes (ITMOs) and a new mechanism under Article 6.4 similar to the Clean Development Mechanism. These carbon markets allow countries to trade emission reductions to help achieve their nationally determined contributions (NDCs).
Statement III: Article 6 also promotes non-market approaches (Article 6.8), which include cooperation through finance, technology transfer, capacity building without involving trading of emission reductions. This allows countries to collaborate on mitigation and adaptation outside carbon markets.
Both market-based (Statement II) and non-market-based (Statement III) mechanisms under Article 6 are critical facets explaining the cooperative approach that Article 6 aims to foster, confirming their explanation of Statement I.
Hence, option A is correct.

q.t:
With reference to Indian National Movement, consider the following pairs:
Person
 Position held
1. Sir Tej Bahadur Sapru
 President, All India Liberal Federation
2. K.C. Neogy
 Member, The Constituent Assembly
3. P.C. Joshi
 General Secretary, Communist Party of India
Which of the pairs given above is/ are correctly matched?

so.t:
Pair l is correctly matched. Tej Bahadur Sapru was the President of All India Liberal Federation and was a prominent member of Liberal Party of India. Sir Tej Bahadur Sapru played a pivotal role in the development of modern India's constitutional and political life. He was a member of the All India Congress Committee, the General Secretary of the Congress party and also the President of the UP Congress Committee. He was also a member of the UP Legislative Council, and thereafter of the Imperial Legislative Council. He became one of the founding members of a party that eventually came to be called The National Liberal Federation of India. He left the Congress in 1919-20, when the Congress took a turn towards mass politics.
Pair 2 is correctly matched. K.C. Neogy was a member of the Constituent Assembly of India, and the member of the first Cabinet of independent India. Neogy was a member of the Indian National Congress and was elected as a member of the Central Legislative Assembly in 1920 representing Bengal. He was the second Finance Minister of free India. The First Finance Commission was constituted under the chairmanship of KC Neogy for the period 1952-57 in the year 1952.
Pair 3 is correctly matched. P.C. Joshi was the first General Secretary of the Communist Party of India. He was one of the leading figures of the communist movement in India. Born in Almora in 1907, Joshi joined the Communist Party in 1929, and rose to prominence thereafter. He was arrested in connection with the Meerut Conspiracy Case.

q.t:
Which the statement(s) is/are not correct for the 9th Schedule of the Constitution of India?
1. It was inserted by the First Amendment in 1951.
2. It includes those laws which are beyond the purview of judicial review.
3. It was inserted by the 42nd Amendment.
4. The laws in the 9th Schedule are primarily those which pertain to the matters of national security.
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:

so.t:
Exp) Option c is the correct answer.
. The 9th schedule was added to the constitution by the Ist amendt. to the constitution in 1951 (Statement 3 is incorrect)
. The Ninth Schedule of the Constitution contains a list of central and state laws which cannot be challenged in
courts. (Statement 4 is incorrect)

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to Cooperative societies in India:
1. They do not fall under the ambit of Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005.
2. They do not fall under the definition of ‘State’ under Article 12 of the Constitution of India.
3. Part IXB of the Constitution of India is applicable to both State and Multi-State cooperative societies.
How many of the statements given above are correct?

so.t:
Explanation:
● Cooperative society is an autonomous body and is not a public authority within the meaning of Section 2(h) of the Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005. Thus, Co-operative societies are not bound to provide information requested by citizens. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Cooperative societies do not fall under the definition of ‘State’ as under Article 12 of the Indian Constitution.
So, statement 2 is correct. :
○ "Cooperative Societies" as a subject is listed in State List under Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of
India. It is listed in Entry 32 of the List II (State List).
● Part IXB was inserted by the 97th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2011. Part IXB is applicable to the Multi-State cooperative societies and the societies within the Union Territories. It does not apply to the local cooperative societies within a State. So, statement 3 is not correct.
So, only two of the statements given above are correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

opt.t: The annexation following Maharaja Ranjit Singh's death (1839)

q.t: Which event marked the beginning of direct British administrative control over Punjab?

so.t:
• The First Anglo-Sikh War (1845-46) resulted in the Treaty of Lahore (1846), which imposed some restrictions on Sikh sovereignty but did not lead to direct British administrative control.
• The Second Anglo-Sikh War (1848-49) ended with a decisive British victory at the Battle of Gujrat in February 1849. Following this, the British East India Company annexed Punjab in March-April 1849, marking the beginning of direct British administrative control over Punjab.
• The Battle of Plassey (1757) and the annexation following Maharaja Ranjit Singh's death (1839) are unrelated to the start of British administration in Punjab.
This annexation ended Sikh rule and brought Punjab under British colonial administration

opt.t: Only Statement I and II are correct
Only Statement II and III are correct
Only Statement I and III are correct

q.t:
A family in Punjab installs a multi-stage water purifier for their bore-well water. Consider these statements about the purification process:
Statement I: Activated carbon filters remove chlorine, pesticides, and organic compounds but cannot remove dissolved salts like fluorides commonly found in Punjab's groundwater. Statement II: Reverse Osmosis (RO) effectively removes heavy metals, dissolved salts, and fluorides, but also removes beneficial minerals like calcium and magnesium.
Statement III: UV purification kills bacteria and viruses but is ineffective against chemical contaminants and requires pre-filtration to remove turbidity.

so.t:
• Statement I: Activated carbon filters remove chlorine, pesticides, and organic compounds effectively from water. However, they cannot remove dissolved salts like fluorides or heavy metals commonly found in Punjab’s groundwater.
• Statement II: Reverse Osmosis (RO) systems effectively remove heavy metals, dissolved salts, fluorides, and various other contaminants by forcing water through a semi-permeable membrane. However, RO also removes beneficial minerals like calcium and magnesium, which may necessitate re-mineralization.
• Statement III: UV purification kills bacteria and viruses by damaging their DNA/RNA but is ineffective against chemical contaminants. UV systems require pre-filtration to remove turbidity because suspended particles can shield microorganisms from UV light, reducing disinfection efficiency

q.t: In the Western world, the balance of power system prevailed in :

so.t: The balance of power system was a defining feature of European international politics in both the 18th and 19th centuries. It aimed to prevent any single state from dominating the continent by forming shifting alliances to maintain equilibrium. In the 18th century, this system was evident during the Wars of Spanish and Austrian Succession, where major powers like Britain, France, and Austria sought to balance each other. In the 19th century, it was formalized after the Napoleonic Wars through the Congress of Vienna (1815) and maintained by the Concert of Europe. Thus, it prevailed in both centuries.

q.t:
With reference to financial grants in India, consider the following statements:
1. Additional Grants are provided when the amount authorized by the budget is found insufficient for the purpose it was sanctioned.
2. Token Grants are issued to meet unanticipated expenditures that were not included in the annual financial statement.
3. Excess Grants are granted to regularize expenditures that exceed the amount initially sanctioned.
Which of the statements given above are correct ?

so.t:
Explanation:
Article 115 of the Indian Constitution provides for different financial grants like supplementary, additional, or excess grants.
Statement 1 is correct:
Additional Grants are provided when the amount authorized by the budget is found insufficient for the purpose it was sanctioned.
Additional Grants are used to supplement the original budget allocation when it's not enough to cover the expenses. This ensures that the government can continue to fund essential services and projects without interruption.
Statement 2 is not correct:
Token grants are provided when funds to meet the proposed expenditure on a new service can be made available by re-appropriation.
A demand for the grant of a token sum (of Re 1) is submitted to the vote of the Lok Sabha and if assented, funds are made available.
Statement 3 is correct:
Excess grants Issued to regularize expenditures incurred over and above the sanctioned amount, after the expenditure has been incurred and examined by the Public Accounts Committee.

q.t:
Regarding Peacock tarantula (Gooty tarantula), consider the following statements:
I. It is an omnivorous crustacean.
II. Its natural habitat in India is only limited to some forest areas. III. In its natural habitat, it is an arboreal species.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
Explanation:
Statement I: Incorrect. The Peacock tarantula (Gooty tarantula), scientifically known as Poecilotheria metallica, is a species of tarantula spider, not an omnivorous crustacean. It is carnivorous, preying mainly on flying insects.
Statement II: Correct. Its natural habitat in India is limited to some forest areas, specifically restricted to deciduous forest regions in Andhra Pradesh and the Eastern Ghats. The species has a very limited and fragmented range, making it critically endangered.
Statement III: Correct. The species is arboreal, meaning it primarily lives in tree hollows and makes its asymmetric funnel webs in tall trees. It is adapted to an arboreal lifestyle.
Thus, statements II and III are correct, while statement I is incorrect.

q.t: In India, which one of the following Constitutional Amendments was widely believed to be enacted to overcome the judicial interpretations of the Fundamental Rights?

so.t:
Exp) Option a is the correct answer
Option a is correct: The First Amendment Act, 1951, added the fourth clause to Article 15 that empowered the government to make any law for the upliftment of socially and educationally backward classes of citizens or for the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes.
